Name: latency_aware_inference_pac_bayes
Title: Latency-Aware Machine Learning Inference Using PAC-Bayes and Spectral Stability
Short Hypothesis: PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds can be leveraged to develop a latency-aware inference mechanism using Jacobian-based layer-skipping, enforcing strict wall-clock budgets while minimizing hidden-state perturbation.
Related Work: Existing work on latency-aware inference focuses on heuristic methods without formal guarantees. PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds have been used separately in optimization and stability analysis but not combined for latency-aware inference. This proposal uniquely integrates these frameworks to provide formal guarantees on latency and stability.
Abstract: This paper proposes a novel latency-aware inference mechanism using PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds. By leveraging Jacobian-based layer-skipping, we enforce strict wall-clock budgets and minimize hidden-state perturbation. Our approach provides formal guarantees on latency and stability, making it suitable for latency-critical applications. We conduct extensive experiments, including latency benchmarks, perturbation-cost accounting, and throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers, to validate our approach. Results show significant improvements in latency and stability compared to existing heuristic methods.

Experiments.name: Latency Benchmarks
Experiments.description: Measure the inference latency of our approach compared to baseline methods under strict wall-clock budgets.

Experiments.name: Perturbation-Cost Accounting
Experiments.description: Quantify the hidden-state perturbation caused by layer-skipping and compare it to baseline methods.

Experiments.name: Throughput-Accuracy Pareto Frontiers
Experiments.description: Evaluate the trade-off between throughput and accuracy for our approach and baseline methods.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The approach may introduce additional computational overhead during training.
The effectiveness of layer-skipping may vary across different architectures and datasets.
Formal guarantees may not hold under extreme latency constraints or highly dynamic environments.
